run.initial_example:
Concept: Compound Interest

Example:
Imagine you plant a single apple tree in your backyard.
Each year, it grows and produces new seeds.
Instead of harvesting all the apples, you let some fall and grow into new trees.
The next year, you have the original tree plus the new ones, and they all produce more seeds.

Starting Point:
  - Initial investment (one tree): $100
  - Annual growth rate: 10%

After 1 year:
  You have $100 + ($100 × 0.10) = $110.
  This is simple interest — you only earn on the original amount.

After 2 years (with compounding):
  You earn 10% on the new total of $110.
  $110 + ($110 × 0.10) = $121.
  The extra $1 compared to simple interest ($120) comes from the "seed" money ($10) you earned last year also growing.

After 3 years:
  You earn 10% on $121.
  $121 + ($121 × 0.10) = $133.10.
  The process repeats, building on itself.

Key Insight:
  Compound interest is growth that builds on its own previous growth. Over time, this creates a much larger result than simply adding the same amount each year, because your earnings start earning too.
